findings.description: Pagination offset uses page*page_size but page is 1-based, so page 1 yields offset=page_size and silently skips the entire first window of results; correct formula is (page-1)*page_size.
name_pattern is a caller-supplied regex compiled and run over every row, allowing catastrophic-backtracking (ReDoS) patterns like '(a+)+$' to exhaust CPU and cause denial of service.
re.compile on an invalid user-supplied pattern raises re.error which is unhandled and propagates as an unrecoverable 500-style failure with no input validation.
The entire non-deleted users table is fetched into memory then filtered, sorted, and sliced in Python regardless of page_size, giving O(N) memory/time that does not scale.
Negative page produces a negative slice offset that silently returns wrong rows from the end of the list instead of an empty/validated result, with no bounds check on page.
display_name is assumed non-null; a NULL display_name yields None and crashes matcher.search(None) and u['display_name'].lower() with TypeError.
page_size is only capped at the top via min(); a zero or negative page_size passes through unchecked and produces an empty or reversed slice window.
departments() returns SELECT DISTINCT department which can include NULL, yielding None inside a declared list[str].
deactivate() opens a fresh connection and commits per call with no transaction batching or WAL configuration, which is inefficient under repeated mutation load.
Optional is imported from typing but PEP 604 'X | None' style is used elsewhere in modern code; mixed typing convention is a minor style inconsistency.
